title: Korean Fried Chicken (Yangnyeom)

subs.name: 500 g cauliflower florets for a vegetarian version — double-fry at the same temperatures
2 tbsp red chilli paste + 1 tsp soy sauce for a rough alternative with milder heat
jaggery syrup (1 tbsp jaggery dissolved in 1 tbsp warm water) or plain sugar

steps.text: Toss the chicken pieces with 3 cloves of minced garlic, grated ginger, salt and black pepper. Rest for 15 minutes while you prepare the coating and sauce.

Combine maida, cornflour and baking powder in a wide bowl. Toss the marinated chicken pieces in the flour mixture, pressing lightly so the coating adheres on all sides. Shake off any excess — a thin, even coat fries better than a thick clump.

Heat oil in a deep kadai to about 170°C (drop a pinch of flour in — it should sizzle steadily and rise to the surface within 3 seconds). Fry the chicken in batches for 8–10 minutes until pale golden and just cooked through. Drain on a wire rack or paper towels.

Raise the oil temperature to 190°C (a piece of chicken dropped in should sizzle vigorously and immediately). Fry the same pieces again for 2–3 minutes until deeply golden and very crisp. Drain well. This second fry drives out remaining moisture, creating the signature crunch.

For the sauce, combine gochujang, soy sauce, honey, vinegar and gingelly oil in a small saucepan over low-medium flame. Add the remaining 2 cloves of minced garlic. Stir continuously for 2 minutes until the sauce is glossy, fragrant and just slightly thickened.

Toss the hot crispy chicken pieces in the warm sauce until every piece is evenly glazed. Work quickly so the crust does not have time to soften. Scatter sesame seeds over the top and serve immediately.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → 500 g cauliflower florets for a vegetarian version — double-fry at the same temperatures; honey → jaggery syrup (1 tbsp jaggery dissolved in 1 tbsp warm water) or plain sugar; gochujang → 2 tbsp red chilli paste + 1 tsp soy sauce for a rough alternative with milder heat
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A deep kadai with at least 8 cm of oil works perfectly for the double-fry — fill it generously so the pieces float freely. The coating uses maida and cornflour (both standard Indian pantry items) along with baking powder to create the airy crunch. Honey can be replaced with jaggery syrup for an earthier sweetness. Gochujang is now stocked at most metro specialty grocery stores; the chilli sauce substitution above works well as a backup.
